text: I am telling her before anybody calls anybody.

Yes. The go-live moved twice, and I am accountable for the date.
That is on me. I should have escalated a week earlier.
I do not have that number now. You will have it by five.
Let me explain what protects the new date.
The go-live moved twice, Diogo. Whose fault is that?
The approval is the cause. And the first week is on me. I should have escalated earlier. I am accountable for the date.
So you admit the project is out of control.
No. I admit one week is mine. I stand by June 27th, and two things protect it.
What does the delay cost us? Give me the number.
I do not have the final number here, and I will not guess it in this room. You will have it by five o'clock.
The client thinks your team is slow. What do I tell them?
Tell them the tests are already running, and the scope is fixed. And I need one thing from this room: their security team on a call this week.
Fine. You get the call. And Diogo, that was a straight answer. Keep it that way.
Yes. I am accountable for the date.
That is on me. I should have escalated earlier.
I stand by June 27th, and two things protect it.
I need one thing from this room.

Diogo, twenty minutes, three directors, and I want you to hear this before you walk in. They are not looking for a perfect project. They are looking for a name, so that somebody in this building is responsible. If you defend yourself, they will smell it, and it will take an hour. So do the opposite. Answer the question in one sentence, before you explain anything. Own the part that is yours, in four words, and then move to the plan. If they ask for a number you do not have, do not invent it -- give them an hour, and send it. And whatever happens, keep your voice steady. The calm person owns that room, and after twenty minutes they will not remember your slides. They will remember whether they can trust you.

Yes. The go-live moved twice, and I am accountable for the date. The reason is the database approval: the client's security team went silent for eleven days, and the same approval took four weeks last year. Now, the part that is on me: I should have escalated a week earlier. I did not, and that first week is mine. Here is where we are. I stand by June 27th, and two things protect it: the tests are already running on the old database, and the scope is now the standard reports only. You asked what the delay costs. I do not have the final number in front of me -- I will not guess it in this room. You will have it by five o'clock today. And there is one thing I need from this committee: the client's security team on a call this week. Without that call, I cannot protect June 27th, and I would rather tell you now than in September.

The committee asks whose fault the delay is. Diogo answers straight, and says the first week is on him. He stands by June 27th, and names the two things that protect it. When they ask for a number he does not have, he refuses to guess and promises it by five o'clock. Finally, he asks the room for the client's security team on a call this week.
